flashcards.front: What is an array in data structures?
flashcards.back: An array is a linear data structure that stores a collection of elements of the same data type in contiguous memory locations, accessible through their indices.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in an array by index?
flashcards.back: O(1) - constant time complexity because arrays provide direct access to any element via its index.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of linear search in an array?
flashcards.back: O(n) - in the worst case, we may need to traverse all elements to find the target element.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of binary search in a sorted array?
flashcards.back: O(log n) - the search space is halved with each comparison, making it very efficient for large sorted arrays.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of inserting an element at the end of an array?
flashcards.back: O(1) - if there is space available at the end, insertion takes constant time.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of inserting an element at the beginning of an array?
flashcards.back: O(n) - all existing elements need to be shifted one position to the right.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of deleting an element from the beginning of an array?
flashcards.back: O(n) - all elements after the deleted element need to be shifted one position to the left.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between static and dynamic arrays?
flashcards.back: Static arrays have a fixed size determined at compile time, while dynamic arrays can resize themselves during runtime to accommodate more elements.

flashcards.front: How is a 2D array stored in memory?
flashcards.back: 2D arrays can be stored in row-major order (row by row) or column-major order (column by column), depending on the programming language.

flashcards.front: What is the formula to calculate the address of an element in a 1D array with base address B and element size S at index i?
flashcards.back: Address = B + (i * S) - for 0-based indexing. For 1-based: Address = B + ((i-1) * S).

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of bubble sort on an array?
flashcards.back: O(n²) - bubble sort uses nested loops, making it inefficient for large arrays.

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of arrays over other data structures for element access?
flashcards.back: Arrays provide O(1) random access time due to contiguous memory allocation and index-based addressing.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of an array of size n?
flashcards.back: O(n) - an array of n elements requires O(n) space to store the elements.

flashcards.front: What is the limitation of arrays for frequent insertions and deletions?
flashcards.back: Arrays have O(n) time complexity for insertions/deletions at arbitrary positions because elements need to be shifted.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for finding element address in a 2D array stored in row-major order?
flashcards.back: Address = B + ((i * n) + j) * S, where B is base address, i is row index, j is column index, n is number of columns, S is element size.
